summary: Rem Koolhaas (full name Remment Lucas Koolhaas) is a Dutch architect from Rotterdam who co-founded the Office of Metropolitan Architecture (OMA) with Elia Zenghelis. He is known for landmark buildings including the Seattle Central Library, the CCTV Headquarters in Beijing, the Casa da Música in Porto, the Qatar National Library, and De Rotterdam. He is also a prominent architectural theorist, authoring 'Delirious New York' and 'S,M,L,XL', and won the 2000 Pritzker Prize.
comprehensive_summary:
Rem Koolhaas (full name Remment Lucas Koolhaas) is a Dutch architect who co-founded the Office of Metropolitan Architecture (OMA), based in his hometown of Rotterdam, with Elia Zenghelis. He is also a professor at Harvard. Koolhaas was a teacher of Zaha Hadid during her studies in London. He also runs AMO, OMA's think tank, which collaborated with designer Virgil Abloh on a 2019 exhibition at the Museum of Contemporary Art titled 'Figures of Speech.'

Koolhaas's most frequently cited building is the Seattle Central Library, a glass-and-steel structure with a diamond-patterned facade. The building includes features such as the Charles Simonyi Mixing Center (also called the 'Mixing Chamber'), a 'Books Spiral' four-story ramp numbered 000–999 for the nonfiction collection, and the 'Living Room' connected to the Mixing Chamber by fluorescent yellow-green escalators. Ring-shaped corridors are painted blood red, and George Legrady's installation 'Making Visible the Invisible' uses six plasma screens displaying live data with diagonal half-white, half-black support columns. The building was designed as five stable platforms connected by four 'in between' planes and has a capacity for 1.45 million items. Joshua Prince-Ramus co-designed the building. Structural engineers Magnusson Klemencic Associates worked on the project.

His second most-cited work is the CCTV Headquarters in Beijing, designed with Ole Scheeren, featuring two slanted towers connected by a 75-meter perpendicular cantilevered section; the building is popularly nicknamed for its resemblance to a pair of shorts (or 'big pants'). OMA also designed the Shenzhen Stock Exchange building in China.

Koolhaas designed the Casa da Música in Porto (also called Casa de Musica), the Qatar National Library in Education City, Doha (completed 2017), which resembles two pieces of paper being separated and folded, and De Rotterdam, described as the largest building in the Netherlands. He designed the McCormick Tribune Campus Center at the Illinois Institute of Technology, topped by a huge tubular metal train tunnel that shields it from the elevated train. He also designed the Guggenheim Hermitage Museum in Las Vegas (a collaboration between the Guggenheim and the Hermitage at the Venetian casino), the RAK Convention Center in the UAE (described as resembling the Death Star), and OMA worked on the Serpentine Galleries pavilion in London in 2006. OMA converted a gin distillery into the Fondazione Prada in Milan, which includes a 'haunted house' covered in gold leaf. OMA also designed a Prada 'Transformer' in Seoul — a movable building in the shape of a tetrahedron with differently shaped faces. OMA designed part of the Leeum (Samsung Museum of Art) in Seoul, alongside Mario Botta and Jean Nouvel. In Taipei, Koolhaas designed a performing arts center with a giant orb sticking out of the side.

As a theorist, Koolhaas wrote 'Delirious New York,' described as a 'retroactive manifesto.' Its cover features his wife Madelon Vriesendorp's painting 'Flagrant Delit,' showing two distorted skyscrapers in bed next to the Statue of Liberty's torch. The book called Manhattan the 'twentieth century's Rosetta Stone,' praises the 'chance-like' nature of city living, and analyzes a project commissioned by the Rockefeller family as a 'double paradox' of congestion and space. His monumental book 'S,M,L,XL,' co-designed with Bruce Mau, arranges OMA's projects in order of size and contains essays such as 'Islam after Einstein,' the poem 'Learning Japanese,' and a masterplan essay for Yokohama described as 'programmatic lava.' It also contains an essay that criticizes Disneyland for making the imperialist assumption that only Westerners can embrace modernity. He also introduced the term 'Manhattanism' to describe an ideology embracing urban congestion. He wrote the essay 'Junkspace,' whose consequences were illustrated in the Barcelona Airport and Madrid Atocha station. Koolhaas won the 2000 Pritzker Prize. His 2002 proposed redesign of the European Union flag, never adopted, is referred to as the 'Barcode' design — praised for being easily updated to reflect new EU member states. OMA also created the 'Image of Europe' exposition as part of its research on rebranding Europe.

works.name: Seattle Central Library
works.description: A glass-and-steel library in Seattle, Washington with a diamond-patterned facade. One of Koolhaas's most cited works. Features: the Charles Simonyi Mixing Chamber; the 'Books Spiral' (a four-story ramp numbered 000–999 for nonfiction); the 'Living Room' connected to the Mixing Chamber by fluorescent yellow-green escalators; ring-shaped corridors painted blood red; and George Legrady's 'Making Visible the Invisible' installation with six plasma screens. Designed as five stable platforms connected by four 'in between' planes. Capacity for 1.45 million items. Co-designed with Joshua Prince-Ramus. Structural engineers Magnusson Klemencic Associates worked on the project.

works.name: S,M,L,XL
works.description: A monumental architectural theory book co-designed with Bruce Mau. Arranges OMA's projects in order of size (Small, Medium, Large, Extra-Large). Contains essays including 'Islam after Einstein,' the poem 'Learning Japanese,' a Yokohama masterplan essay using 'programmatic lava,' and an essay criticizing Disneyland as imperialist. Introduced the term 'Manhattanism.'

works.description: Koolhaas's architectural 'retroactive manifesto' about Manhattan. Called Manhattan the 'twentieth century's Rosetta Stone.' Its cover features his wife Madelon Vriesendorp's painting 'Flagrant Delit,' showing two distorted skyscrapers in bed next to the Statue of Liberty's torch. Praises the 'chance-like' nature of city living. Analyzes a Rockefeller-commissioned project as a 'double paradox' of congestion and space.

works.name: OMA (Office for Metropolitan Architecture)
works.description: Architecture firm co-founded by Koolhaas with Elia Zenghelis, based in Rotterdam. Known for incubating 'starchitects' including Joshua Prince-Ramus (who founded REX) and Bjarke Ingels (who founded BIG). Collaborated with Cecil Balmond on the Maison a Bordeaux. Koolhaas also runs AMO, OMA's think tank.

works.name: EU Barcode Flag Proposal
works.description: Koolhaas's 2002 never-adopted redesign of the European Union flag, referred to by its resemblance to a barcode. Praised for being easily updated to reflect new member states. OMA also created the 'Image of Europe' exposition to research rebranding Europe.

works.name: Biographical
works.description: Rem Koolhaas (Remment Lucas Koolhaas) is a Dutch architect and theorist from Rotterdam. He won the 2000 Pritzker Prize. He is a professor at Harvard. He was a teacher of Zaha Hadid. He wrote the essay 'Junkspace' (whose consequences are illustrated by the Barcelona Airport and Madrid Atocha station). De Rotterdam is described as the largest building in the Netherlands.

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Other buildings by Koolhaas or OMA include: the McCormick Tribune Campus Center at IIT (topped by a tubular metal train tunnel); the Guggenheim Hermitage Museum in Las Vegas (a Guggenheim–Hermitage collaboration at the Venetian casino); the RAK Convention Center in the UAE (resembling the Death Star); the Shenzhen Stock Exchange; the Fondazione Prada in Milan (a converted gin distillery with a 'haunted house' covered in gold leaf); a Prada 'Transformer' in Seoul (a movable tetrahedron with differently shaped faces); the Serpentine Galleries pavilion in London (2006); part of the Leeum Samsung Museum of Art in Seoul (alongside Botta and Nouvel); and the Taipei Performing Arts Center (with a giant orb sticking out of the side).
